content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To       :  File                                    Date  :  January 19, 1989


From  :  FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
         John McLean

Subject :  Advice No. T-88-446


          In December, I spoke to Gordon Phillips regarding this advice
request.  I informed him of the following:

     1.   We are providing advice under the regulation as adopted
by the Commission on December 6.

     2.   The cost of producing Xmas cards is included in the
phrase "sent at public expense" under the mass mailing law.

     3.   If each individual councilmember sends out cards with his
or her name printed inside, each member may send out 199 cards.

          Mr. Phillips indicated that he did not need written
confirmation of this advice.

JGM:ld:jimmemo1

GORDON C. PHILLIPS
CITY ATTORNEY

TELEPHONE
(213) 372-1171

FPPC
Nov 23 8 40 AM '88

CITY OF REDONDO BEACH
CALIFORNIA

415 DIAMOND STREET
REDONDO BEACH, CALIFORNIA 90277

November 22, 1988


VIA FAX AND FEDERAL EXPRESS


State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California

Re: Proposition 73 Advise

The City of Redondo Beach is contemplating printing
Christmas cards for its elected officials. The individual name
of each official would appear on each card. The number of cards
would depend on the request of each elected official. In some
instances more than 200 cards would be printed, in other
instances less than 200 cards would be printed.

The cards would then be sent at the expense of the
individual office holder.


ISSUES

1.   Is the expense of the production of the Christmas cards
a Proposition 73 expense under the current regulations?

2.   Is the answer to No. 1 the same if each official is
limited to 199 cards?

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
November 22, 1988
Page 2

     3.     How is the 200 piece limitation determined, i.e. by the
aggregate of cards printed or by the number allocated to each
individual elected official?

     4.     If the printing of the cards is not limited by
Proposition 73 under the current regulations, what would be the
effect of an intervening regulation prohibiting the mailing of
the cards, after the printing of the cards have been printed?

     Because of the timeliness of this request, I am requesting
your earliest response.


                              Very truly yours,


                              [signature]

                              Gordon C. Phillips

                              City Attorney


GCP:N

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 28, 1988


Gordon C. Phillips
City Attorney
415 Diamond Street
Redondo Beach, CA  90277

Re:  88-446

Dear Mr. Phillips:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform
Act was received on November 22, 1988 by the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact John McLean, an attorney in the
Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly. Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions,
or more information is needed, you should expect a response
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written
advice. If more information is needed, the person assigned to
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to
advise you as to information needed. If your request is for
informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can.
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. sec.
18329).)

     You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        Kathryn L. Duncan
                                        Diane M. Griffiths    /jt
                                        General Counsel

                    Re:  Proposition 73 Advise

Christmas cards for its elected officials.  The individual name
of each official would appear on each card.  The number of cards
would depend on the request of each elected official.  In some
instances more than 200 cards would be printed, in other
instances less than 200 cards would be printed.

     The cards would then be sent at the expense of the
individual office holder.


     ISSUES

     1.    Is the expense of the production of the Christmas cards
a Proposition 73 expense under the current regulations?

     2.    Is the answer to No. 1 the same if each official is
limited to 199 cards?

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
November 22, 1988
Page 2


3. How is the 200 piece limitation determined, i.e. by the
aggregate of cards printed or by the number allocated to each
individual elected official?

4. If the printing of the cards is not limited by
Proposition 73 under the current regulations, what would be the
effect of an intervening regulation prohibiting the mailing of
the cards, after the printing of the cards have been printed?

Because of the timeliness of this request, I am requesting

Very truly yours,

[signature]

Gordon C. Phillips
City Attorney

sections.question: 1. Is the expense of the production of the Christmas cards a Proposition 73 expense under the current regulations? 2. Is the answer to No. 1 the same if each official is limited to 199 cards? 3. How is the 200 piece limitation determined, i.e. by the aggregate of cards printed or by the number allocated to each individual elected official? 4. If the printing of the cards is not limited by Proposition 73 under the current regulations, what would be the effect of an intervening regulation prohibiting the mailing of the cards, after the printing of the cards have been printed?
sections.conclusion: 1. We are providing advice under the regulation as adopted by the Commission on December 6. 2. The cost of producing Xmas cards is included in the phrase "sent at public expense" under the mass mailing law. 3. If each individual councilmember sends out cards with his or her name printed inside, each member may send out 199 cards.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the costs of producing and sending Christmas cards bearing individual councilmembers' names constitute reportable expenses under Proposition 73, and how the 200-card limitation under the mass mailing law applies to each official's distribution.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC determined that Christmas card production costs are subject to Proposition 73 reporting requirements as public expenses, and that each individual councilmember may send out 199 cards when the cards bear that member's individual name.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains incoming request (dated Nov 22, 1988), acknowledgment letter (Nov 28, 1988), and informal advice memorandum (Jan 19, 1989). The memorandum indicates verbal advice was provided in December 1988 without written confirmation requested. Extraction based on the final informal advice memorandum which provides the FPPC's substantive conclusions.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Is the expense of the production of the Christmas cards a Proposition 73 expense under the current regulations? 2. Is the answer to No. 1 the same if each official is limited to 199 cards? 3. How is the 200 piece limitation determined, i.e. by the aggregate of cards printed or by the number allocated to each individual elected official? 4. If the printing of the cards is not limited by Proposition 73 under the current regulations, what would be the effect of an intervening regulation prohibiting the mailing of the cards, after the printing of the cards have been printed?

CONCLUSION: 1. We are providing advice under the regulation as adopted by the Commission on December 6. 2. The cost of producing Xmas cards is included in the phrase "sent at public expense" under the mass mailing law. 3. If each individual councilmember sends out cards with his or her name printed inside, each member may send out 199 cards.

embedding.summary: The City of Redondo Beach sought FPPC advice regarding whether Christmas cards bearing elected officials' names would constitute reportable Proposition 73 expenses. The FPPC responded that card production costs are public expenses subject to reporting, and each official may distribute up to 199 cards.
